core_identity.title: Probability Basics: The Horse Race (Two Dice)
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: two dice probability space and relative likelihood
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Compare theoretical and experimental probability for two dice by completing a 6×6 probability space, explaining why some totals are more likely, and evaluating how experimental data matches theory over time.

verse_ality.driving_question: Why are some totals more likely than others when we roll two dice, and how does experimental data compare to the theoretical model?

knowing_world.focus: Distinguish theoretical vs experimental probability using the two-dice sample space, counting outcomes for each total and explaining why 7 is the most common sum.
key_concepts: Sample space (36 equally likely outcomes for two fair dice)
Why some outcomes are more likely (number of combinations)
activities: Complete the 6×6 probability space by adding the two dice in each cell.
Count how many ways each total (2 to 12) can occur and compare likelihoods.
Explain the pattern in the sums (why totals near 7 are more common).
tools: Interactive: Horse Race Web App (two dice) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG6Xuh_wBA/qvvIquyQgbouDylLe3iRVw/view?embed

human_story.focus: Explore how probability is used to design fair systems and make decisions (weather, medicine, games), and how misunderstanding probability can lead to unfair or biased outcomes in real-world systems.
justice_prompts: Whose lives are affected when real-world outcomes don’t reflect theoretical models?
How can we design systems that are fair, transparent, and evidence-based?

expressive_self.focus: Use story and metaphor to explain why outcomes differ in likelihood even in a fair system, building an intuitive feel for ‘common’ and ‘long shot’ events.
activities: Imagine each total has a personality (e.g., Horse 7 vs Horse 2). What kind of ‘person’ is each one?
Draw a comic or write a short story about the race and how the horses feel about their chances.
Explain (in your own words) why some outcomes are more likely than others, even when everything is fair.

designed_world.focus: Run an experiment using the interactive horse race, record frequencies of totals, and compare the experimental results to the theoretical probabilities from the probability space.
design_challenges: Before starting, make a prediction: Which horse (total) will win?
As you play, record how many times each total appears.
Compare the experimental results to the theoretical probabilities and describe what changes over time.
Buddy challenge: compare predictions and outcomes with a classmate; discuss why results differ.
Design prompt: how would you redesign the game for someone who can’t use a mouse or who uses a screen reader?

regulation_supports: Breathing reset: breathe in for “Expect”, breathe out for “Observe”; focus on noticing patterns rather than outcomes.
Body check-in: notice how you feel when your prediction is wrong and what shows up in your body.
Finger tap: tap from 2 to 12 slowly; which totals feel stable and which feel like long shots?

pathways.name: Final Task: The Race Report
pathways.focus: Complete a two-dice probability model and compare it to experimental results from the horse race, then suggest an inclusive design improvement.
products: A completed probability space diagram showing all 36 dice combinations.
A table showing the number of times each total appeared during your race.
A short explanation comparing experimental results to theoretical probabilities.
A design idea to make the game more inclusive or informative.
criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explain the difference between theoretical and experimental probability and why totals have different likelihoods.
Fluency & Accuracy – Complete the probability space and track results accurately.
Application to Problems – Analyse outcomes and interpret patterns in the data.
Independence & Reflection – Reflect on unpredictability and propose a realistic improvement.

required: Understand that a fair die has equally likely outcomes
Basic fraction/ratio thinking (e.g., outcomes out of 36)
Ability to record frequencies in a simple table

builds_toward: Relative frequency and convergence with more trials
Risk, bias, and fairness when probability is used in real systems
